Superhuman accessibility program Making Superhuman work for everyone

Published Written
Updated Changed

Building the accessibility practice from the ground up: audit frameworks, ARIA patterns, keyboard navigation, and engineering mentorship across the product. I helped build a plan to make Superhuman easier to use for people with disabilities. This included keyboard shortcuts, screen reader support, and teaching engineers how to do it.

When I joined Superhuman, accessibility wasn't ignored so much as undefined. There was no audit process, no shared language for what "accessible" meant in the context of a keyboard-first email client, and no clear owner. My first job was to make the invisible visible: catalog every interaction, measure it against WCAG 2.2, and build a prioritized backlog that engineering could actually work against.[1]

The hardest part wasn't the technical work. It was building consensus that accessibility deserved sprint time alongside feature work. That meant showing up in planning meetings with data, not just principles.[2]

Changelog What changed

2026.03.01

Ongoing work.

References Where I learned this

  • [1]

    WCAG 2.2 became a W3C Recommendation in October 2023.

    Return to text Go back
  • [2]

    Marcy Sutton's work on accessibility testing automation was a key influence on our audit tooling.
